asthmaControl.name: Asthma Control Test (ACT)
asthmaControl.description: Calculate your ACT score — assess asthma control over the past 4 weeks.

meta.title: Asthma Control Test (ACT) Calculator — Score 5–25 | Health Calculators
meta.description: Take the validated Asthma Control Test online. 5 questions, score 5–25, classification: well / not well / poorly controlled. Free, instant, anonymous.

asthmaControl.title: Asthma Control Test (ACT)
asthmaControl.description: Answer five questions about the past four weeks. Your ACT score (5–25) helps you and your doctor judge how well your asthma is controlled.
asthmaControl.introNote: Reference period: the past 4 weeks. Pick exactly one answer per question.

activityLimitation.title: 1. In the past 4 weeks, how much of the time did your asthma keep you from getting as much done at work, school, or home?

shortnessOfBreath.title: 2. During the past 4 weeks, how often have you had shortness of breath?

nightSymptoms.title: 3. During the past 4 weeks, how often did your asthma symptoms (wheezing, coughing, shortness of breath, chest tightness) wake you up at night or earlier than usual?

rescueInhalerUse.title: 4. During the past 4 weeks, how often have you used your rescue inhaler or nebulizer (e.g. albuterol/salbutamol)?

selfRating.title: 5. How would you rate your asthma control during the past 4 weeks?

hints.wellControlled: Score 20–25: Your asthma appears well controlled. Stay on your current treatment and trigger avoidance. Discuss any step-down with your doctor at the next visit.
hints.notWellControlled: Score 16–19: Your asthma is not adequately controlled. Schedule a visit with your doctor — a step-up in controller therapy is often appropriate.
hints.poorlyControlled: Score 5–15: Your asthma is poorly controlled. Seek medical attention soon. For acute breathlessness use your rescue inhaler and call emergency services if needed.

asthmaControl.incompleteHint: Answer all 5 questions to see your ACT score.

scaleRows.label: Not well controlled
scaleRows.hint: Discuss therapy adjustment with your doctor

asthmaControl.howItWorksText: The Asthma Control Test (ACT) was validated by Nathan and colleagues in 2004 (J Allergy Clin Immunol). Five questions, scored 1–5 each, sum to 5–25 total. A score ≥ 20 indicates well-controlled asthma; ≤ 19 suggests treatment review. GINA recommends the ACT as a longitudinal monitoring tool.
asthmaControl.disclaimer: The ACT is a screening and monitoring tool. It does not replace clinical diagnosis. For acute breathlessness, rescue inhaler use exceeding 4× per day, or rapid deterioration, seek medical attention immediately.

faq.q: What does the Asthma Control Test (ACT) measure?
faq.a: The ACT measures how well your asthma was controlled over the past 4 weeks. It captures activity limitation, shortness of breath, nighttime symptoms, rescue inhaler use, and your subjective control rating.

faq.q: What do the score ranges mean?
faq.a: 20–25 points: well controlled. 16–19 points: not well controlled. 5–15 points: poorly controlled. A score of 25 indicates complete control.

faq.q: How often should I take the ACT?
faq.a: Every 3 months when stable; more often during therapy changes or worsening. Trends across multiple tests carry more weight than a single value.

faq.q: What does a score below 20 mean?
faq.a: A score below 20 indicates your asthma was not optimally controlled in the past 4 weeks. That is a reason to discuss therapy adjustments or trigger avoidance with your doctor — not necessarily an emergency.

faq.q: Is the ACT suitable for children?
faq.a: The ACT shown here is validated for adults and adolescents 12+ years. For children aged 4–11, use the Childhood Asthma Control Test (cACT) with age-appropriate items.

faq.q: Does the ACT replace lung function testing?
faq.a: No. The ACT captures symptoms, not lung function. Spirometry (FEV1) or peak flow measurements complement the ACT — together they give the full picture of asthma control.

faq.q: What if I have acute breathlessness despite a high ACT score?
faq.a: An acute attack overrides the ACT. Use your rescue inhaler (2–4 puffs of albuterol/salbutamol), sit upright, breathe calmly. If symptoms do not improve, call emergency services.
